title: Self-Critique Prompts
concept: Instructing a model to find flaws in its previous reasoning or identify assumptions it made.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, an agent used a weather tool. What is a good self-reflection question after getting the result?

options.text: Did this action work and return the expected data?
What color should my answer be?
How many words can I write?

exercises.explain: Reflecting on action outcomes means checking whether a tool actually worked and gave the data you expected. That is the exact habit we practiced last time!

cards.title: Catch Your Own Mistakes
cards.body: Imagine an AI just gave an answer. What if you asked it to hunt for its own errors before moving on? That simple move can turn a shaky answer into a strong one.

cards.title: What Is a Self-Critique Prompt?
cards.body: A self-critique prompt tells a model to look back at its own reasoning and find flaws or hidden assumptions. Instead of trusting its first answer, it plays its own tough reviewer.

cards.title: Like Proofreading an Essay
cards.body: When you finish an essay, you reread it to spot typos and weak points. A self-critique prompt makes the AI reread its own work and mark the parts that might be wrong.

cards.title: A Prompt in Action
cards.body: You ask: 'List any assumptions you made and any steps that could be wrong.' The AI replies: 'I assumed the store was open Sunday, which I never checked.' Now you know where the risk is.

cards.title: Myth: One Answer Is Always Enough
cards.body: Some think an AI's first answer must be right. But models can quietly make assumptions. Asking it to critique itself often reveals errors you would have missed.

cards.title: First Draft vs. Self-Critique
cards.body: See the difference when a model reviews its own reasoning.

left.text: AI gives an answer and stops, hidden assumptions and all.

right.text: AI lists its assumptions and flags steps that might be wrong.

exercises.prompt: Which prompt best asks a model to self-critique its reasoning?

options.text: What flaws or assumptions were in your previous answer?
Make your answer twice as long.
Tell me a fun fact about cats.
Repeat your answer word for word.

exercises.explain: A self-critique prompt asks the model to search its own reasoning for flaws and assumptions. Only option A does that.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A self-critique prompt asks the model to identify assumptions it made in its previous reasoning.
exercises.explain: That's exactly right. Self-critique prompts push the model to surface hidden assumptions and possible mistakes in its earlier reasoning.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the self-critique prompt.
exercises.sentence: Please review your last answer and list every assumption you made.
exercises.explain: A self-critique prompt asks the model to name each assumption it made, so 'assumption' is the word that fits.

exercises.prompt: Which response looks like an AI performing self-critique on its own reasoning?

options.text: On reflection, I assumed the data was from 2024 without confirming, which could make my estimate wrong.
My answer is perfect and needs no changes at all.
I don't feel like checking my work today.

exercises.explain: Real self-critique names a specific assumption and explains why it might cause an error, just like option A does.

exercises.prompt: An AI planned a road trip and said 'Drive 2 hours to the beach and arrive by noon.' You want it to self-critique. Which follow-up prompt is best AND why?

options.text: 'What assumptions did you make and which steps could be wrong?' — because it makes the AI surface hidden assumptions like traffic or start time.
'Say the plan again.' — because repeating catches all errors.
'Make the trip sound more exciting.' — because style fixes mistakes.
'Ignore any problems.' — because the first plan is always correct.

exercises.explain: A self-critique prompt asks the model to hunt for its own flaws and assumptions, like assuming no traffic or a certain start time. That's why option A works: it forces the AI to review its reasoning and reveal risks, turning a shaky plan into a trustworthy one.
